scenarioStory.history: 38-year-old male with known epilepsy was working at his desk when his coworker heard him fall to the floor. Coworker found him on the floor with generalized tonic-clonic activity that lasted approximately 3 minutes before stopping spontaneously just as EMS arrived. Patient is now post-ictal — eyes open to voice, mumbling, GCS 11 and slowly improving. Coworker states the patient has a long history of seizures and recently ran out of his Keppra (levetiracetam) about 5 days ago and has not refilled it. No reported trauma to the head during the fall — coworker saw him slump rather than fall hard. No fever, no recent illness, no alcohol or drug use known. While the crew is setting up monitoring, the patient has a SECOND generalized tonic-clonic seizure that does not stop after 2 minutes of continuous activity — meeting status epilepticus criteria.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: Crew arrives 4 minutes after dispatch to find a 38-year-old male supine on the office floor in a post-ictal state. Coworker reports the seizure was generalized tonic-clonic, lasted about 3 minutes, and stopped just as EMS pulled up. Patient is mumbling, opens eyes to voice, GCS 11 and slowly trending up. Vitals are stable. While the crew is mid-assessment — taking baseline vitals, applying SpO2, asking about meds — the patient suddenly stiffens and begins another generalized tonic-clonic seizure. This is now a recurrent seizure without recovery between, and as it continues past 2 minutes of unrelenting activity the crew is in status epilepticus territory per protocol definition. The decision-dense window opens here: airway protection, oxygenation, IV/IO vs IM/IN benzo, and the dose math all need to happen simultaneously while the patient is convulsing. → Benzo given; seizure stops: Crew gave midazolam 10 mg IM promptly. Convulsive activity terminates approximately 90 seconds after the injection. Patient is now post-ictal again, but more deeply suppressed than after the first seizure — GCS 9, respirations slowing to 8/min, SpO2 trending down to 90% on room air. This is the expected post-benzo + post-ictal respiratory depression. Crew must now BVM-assist ventilations, maintain airway adjunct, establish IV access in a non-seizing patient, and prepare for possible recurrence (second dose may be needed). Transport is initiated. → Stable to ED: Patient delivered to the ED with BVM-assisted ventilation, NPA in place, IV established, and no further seizure activity. Crew gives a clean handoff to the ED team — they took an obvious 'IV access in a convulsing patient' bait and chose the IM route, recognized status epilepticus by the 2-minute mark of the recurrent seizure, gave the right dose by the right route, and managed the predictable post-benzo respiratory depression with BVM and capnography. The teaching points sit cleanly: recurrent-without-recovery meets status criteria; IM/IN before IV in the seizing patient; post-benzo respiratory monitoring is mandatory.

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: Crew arrives 4 minutes after dispatch to find a 38-year-old male supine on the office floor in a post-ictal state. Coworker reports the seizure was generalized tonic-clonic, lasted about 3 minutes, and stopped just as EMS pulled up. Patient is mumbling, opens eyes to voice, GCS 11 and slowly trending up. Vitals are stable. While the crew is mid-assessment — taking baseline vitals, applying SpO2, asking about meds — the patient suddenly stiffens and begins another generalized tonic-clonic seizure. This is now a recurrent seizure without recovery between, and as it continues past 2 minutes of unrelenting activity the crew is in status epilepticus territory per protocol definition. The decision-dense window opens here: airway protection, oxygenation, IV/IO vs IM/IN benzo, and the dose math all need to happen simultaneously while the patient is convulsing.
relatedChecklist: Obtain seizure history: duration, onset, meds, anticonvulsant compliance
Position airway, suction frothy secretions, NPA if gag present — NO bite block
Apply SpO2 and waveform capnography; high-flow O2 via NRB
Finger-stick glucose — rule out hypoglycemia as seizure cause
Recognize recurrent seizure / status epilepticus — treat NOW, don't wait
Choose route — IM/IN preferred if no IV; do not delay benzo for IV attempts
Midazolam 10 mg IM (80 kg × 0.2 mg/kg = 16 mg → cap at 10 mg max)
If IV already in place: Midazolam 4 mg IV (80 kg × 0.1 mg/kg = 8 mg → cap at 4 mg)

scenarioEvents.name: Benzo given; seizure stops
scenarioEvents.description: Crew gave midazolam 10 mg IM promptly. Convulsive activity terminates approximately 90 seconds after the injection. Patient is now post-ictal again, but more deeply suppressed than after the first seizure — GCS 9, respirations slowing to 8/min, SpO2 trending down to 90% on room air. This is the expected post-benzo + post-ictal respiratory depression. Crew must now BVM-assist ventilations, maintain airway adjunct, establish IV access in a non-seizing patient, and prepare for possible recurrence (second dose may be needed). Transport is initiated.
relatedChecklist: BVM-assist ventilations at 10-12/min with PEEP; titrate to SpO2 94-98%
Continuous waveform capnography; target EtCO2 35-40 mmHg
Establish IV access NOW that patient is still — prep for potential redose
Prep second midazolam dose; redose if seizure recurs (max 2 benzo doses)
Initiate transport; reassess en route; notify ED of status epilepticus

scenarioEvents.name: Stable to ED
scenarioEvents.description: Patient delivered to the ED with BVM-assisted ventilation, NPA in place, IV established, and no further seizure activity. Crew gives a clean handoff to the ED team — they took an obvious 'IV access in a convulsing patient' bait and chose the IM route, recognized status epilepticus by the 2-minute mark of the recurrent seizure, gave the right dose by the right route, and managed the predictable post-benzo respiratory depression with BVM and capnography. The teaching points sit cleanly: recurrent-without-recovery meets status criteria; IM/IN before IV in the seizing patient; post-benzo respiratory monitoring is mandatory.
relatedChecklist: Structured handoff: recurrent seizure, status met, midazolam 10 mg IM, response
Document glucose, full vitals, time of seizure onset/cessation, and meds given

scenarioEvents.name: IV chase; seizure continues
scenarioEvents.description: Crew attempts repeated IV sticks on a convulsing patient. The muscle activity wrecks every attempt; the patient continues seizing for 4 more minutes — now ~6 minutes total of unrelenting convulsive activity. SpO2 drifts down to 84%, skin cyanotic around lips, oral secretions pooling. The crew has effectively chosen the worst path: no benzo given, no IV obtained, airway worsening. Recovery requires abandoning the IV attempt, giving an IM/IN dose immediately, and aggressive airway management with BVM and high-flow O2.
relatedChecklist: Stop chasing IV; switch to IM/IN route per protocol preference
Midazolam 10 mg IM NOW (80 kg × 0.2 mg/kg = 16 mg → cap at 10 mg max)
Suction airway, NPA, high-flow O2; prep BVM-assist as seizure terminates
Recognize benzo more than two doses risk; consult medical direction if needed

scenarioEvents.name: Underdosed IM benzo
scenarioEvents.description: Crew correctly recognized the need for IM benzo and skipped the IV attempt — but pulled the wrong dose math, giving only 4 mg IM (the IV/IO ceiling dose) instead of the IM ceiling of 10 mg. Seizure does not terminate. After waiting 3-4 minutes for an effect that never comes, the crew must recognize the dosing error, redose to make up the deficit (an additional 6 mg IM to reach the IM ceiling), and accept they will likely hit the protocol's two-dose ceiling — meaning the second dose decision needs to be deliberate and well-documented.
relatedChecklist: Recognize underdosing: IM ceiling is 10 mg, not the IV 4 mg
Midazolam additional 6 mg IM to reach 10 mg total IM ceiling
Suction, NPA, high-flow O2, BVM-assist for hypoxia
Document both doses, times, routes; flag the under-dose for QA review

BVM-assist ventilations at 10-12/min with PEEP; titrate to SpO2 94-98%

Midazolam 10 mg IM NOW (80 kg × 0.2 mg/kg = 16 mg → cap at 10 mg max)
Suction airway, NPA, high-flow O2; prep BVM-assist as seizure terminates
Recognize benzo more than two doses risk; consult medical direction if needed
Recognize underdosing: IM ceiling is 10 mg, not the IV 4 mg
Midazolam additional 6 mg IM to reach 10 mg total IM ceiling
Suction, NPA, high-flow O2, BVM-assist for hypoxia
Document both doses, times, routes; flag the under-dose for QA review
